chapters.title: Close relationships can lift you up or hold you back
chapters.text:
Relationships are the source of our greatest joy and our deepest wounds. People in healthy relationships tend to be happier and more likely to succeed, and the opposite is just as true. A painful relationship can box you in and hold you back.

Choose a partner carefully if you want to enjoy long term success.

The pattern shows up in every kind of close bond, but it is most obvious in romantic relationships, where two people get closer to each other than they get to almost anyone else.

Given how much relationships shape our lives, it pays to know how to keep them healthy. Part of that work is removing anything that drags them down, and anxiety is one of the biggest drag factors. This summary focuses on anxiety in relationships and how to handle it.

You will learn what anxiety does to you and to your partner, and how to manage it when it shows up. Negative emotions and jealousy also get into intimate relationships, and later chapters cover practical ways to deal with them on both sides of the bond. We will also look at attachment styles and the way good listening can change a relationship for the better.

chapters.title: A little anxiety is normal, and it can be managed
chapters.text:
Newlyweds and long term couples will almost certainly hit rough patches, and anxiety is one of the most common causes. Anxiety can whisper that your partner no longer likes you, that they have lost interest, or that someone else has taken your place.

Anxiety makes you suspicious of every move your partner makes. You start seeing signs in things that mean nothing, and the worry grows. A simple example is a woman working late to meet a deadline, and her partner reading it as a sign that she is seeing someone else. At its worst, anxiety can lead to stalking, snooping through devices, and a constant need to know what the other person is doing at every moment.

It is normal for anxious feelings to surface from time to time in a relationship.

Whichever way the anxiety shows up, it should not be allowed to run the show. The skill is to keep it in check, since letting it loose can turn a happy relationship sour. No one likes to be accused of something they did not do, and the suspicion can become a self fulfilling prophecy.

There are practical ways to share your feelings with your partner so the insecurity loses its grip and the relationship stays healthy. The rest of this summary walks through them.

You do not have to be the anxious one in the relationship. Living with an anxious partner can be exhausting, and the partner is rarely acting on purpose. The summary also covers the right response and posture for the calmer partner, so the relationship does not get hurt by reactions that come from a place of frustration.

chapters.title: Insecurity is the engine of relationship anxiety
chapters.text:
Feeling inadequate or unworthy is one of the main drivers of relationship anxiety. Sometimes the feeling comes from a real situation. Sometimes it comes from imagined fear. When the cause is real, the partner is doing something that feeds the insecurity, and the only way to find it is to look honestly at your own thoughts. Once the source is clear, the next step is to talk about it.

When the cause of the insecurity is real, talking it through with your partner is the right move.

How you talk about it matters. Blaming your partner or making accusations you cannot back up will hurt the relationship, and it is worth resisting the urge to do either. The healthier move is to stay calm and collected.

Many anxious people struggle to say what they are feeling, and that is normal. If you can see the physical, behavioral, or emotional signs of anxiety in your partner, the kindest thing you can do is gently invite them to open up. When an anxious person is trying to explain, the best gift is a listening ear. Try to listen without getting defensive.

chapters.title: Your attachment style can build or break the relationship
chapters.text:
Attachment styles are formed early, based on the kind of relationship a child had with a primary caregiver. Knowing your own style, and your partner, is a good way to understand each other better and to strengthen the relationship. Psychologists have grouped attachment into four categories, and it helps to figure out which one fits you and which one fits your partner.

Secure attachment. People in this group had a stable, supportive childhood. They saw their parents as a base from which to go out and explore the world, and they bring that steadiness into adult relationships. They are supportive of their partners and willing to ask for help when they need it.

Anxious preoccupied attachment. People with this style often look to their partner to fill in a sense of being complete. They can struggle with identity, and they tend to be hard to satisfy in a relationship.

Dismissive avoidant attachment. People in this group tend to downplay the importance of close bonds and can pull away from the people they love. They are the ones who insist that they do not care when a partner threatens to leave, even when their behavior in the relationship is harmful.

Fearful avoidant attachment. People in this group live in a kind of tension. They believe that closeness is necessary for a real connection, but they are also afraid of being hurt. They tend to experience emotional swings and find it hard to be open with a partner about what they need.

A secure attachment pattern leaves a person confident, self possessed, and able to connect with others while looking after their own needs, as Theresa Miller has written.

Attachment styles are set in childhood, and they can be shaped by past relationships. Someone who was once secure can become fearful avoidant after a partner cheats, for example. The reverse is also true if they meet a partner who helps them feel safe again. Whatever your style is right now, becoming more secure is still possible.

Anxious people benefit from learning to communicate clearly with their partner, and good communication reduces anxiety for both sides.

chapters.title: Good listening is the fastest way out of a fight
chapters.text:
Conflict is a normal part of any relationship. No matter how long two people have been together, something will eventually spark a disagreement. Putting two humans together and expecting constant peace is unrealistic, and a big reason for the arguments is that both partners are being honest and open with each other. When people are being real, friction is almost inevitable, since no two people are ever the same. Conflict is also a way to learn more about each other.

Long, heated fights between people who have been together for a while are a different story. Some friction is normal, but it should not last forever. Over time, partners should know each other well enough to work through the rough patches easily. That is the theory. In practice, close to half of all marriages in the United States end in divorce.

The reality is that some people simply have not learned the skills for handling hard conversations.

The roots of conflict run deep, and they sit in how emotionally safe each person feels.

If arguments in your relationship keep getting heated, before you blame your partner, make sure you have the skills for resolving the conflict in the first place.

One of those skills is good listening. Arguments are full of emotion, and people will keep talking until they feel heard. The best thing you can do when your partner is angry is to stay quiet and listen. It is hard at first, but it gets easier with practice, and you will be glad you did it. While you listen, try to go beyond the words and read the nonverbal cues your partner is giving you.

When you really listen to your partner, you are showing them that you care, as Theresa Miller has written.

A 2014 study from the University of North Carolina at Greensboro found that American women who married or moved in with a partner at eighteen had a divorce rate of about sixty percent, while those who waited until twenty three had a divorce rate closer to thirty percent.

chapters.title: Every relationship goes through five stages
chapters.text:
Long term relationships tend to pass through five stages. If you are committed, engaged, or married, the framework is worth knowing. The stages are not optional, and many couples worry that something is wrong with them or with their partner when the relationship moves from one stage to the next. Knowing the stages ahead of time can keep a relationship from getting stuck in a toxic place.

The romance stage. This is usually the first stage, and it can last anywhere from two months to two years. The couple is high on the in love feeling, and the partner can seem like a flawless, perfect human. The phrase love is blind probably comes from this stage.

The power struggle stage. As the high of the romance stage fades, the couple starts to notice the rough edges and the differences. Those differences can lead to a power struggle, since both people try to push the other to become their ideal partner. A lot of relationships end in this stage. To survive it, the couple has to learn to agree to disagree, or to find a middle ground where both can be happy.

The stability stage. This is the natural follow up to the power struggle. The couple is no longer surprised by the differences, and they are learning to live with them and to keep the relationship as happy as possible.

The commitment stage. Ideally, this is the stage where a couple gets married, but many couples tie the knot in the romance stage and regret it when they hit the power struggle. In the commitment stage, the couple is happy with the deepening bond. They are starting to find a balance between love, belonging, power, freedom, and fun. They begin to reconnect with the friendships and commitments they may have set aside in the romance stage.

The co creation stage. This is the final stage, and not every couple reaches it. Couples who do reach it have become a real team. They are ready to take on bigger projects together, such as raising children, building a family business, or facing a serious illness, as a single unit.

The romance stage is short, but its purpose is to give the couple a taste of what the relationship can become if they keep working on it.

The pace at which couples move through the stages varies a lot, and it is normal to move back and forth between them.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Long term relationships can lose their spark over time. What should you do when the spark fades and you start losing interest in your partner? First, know that it is a normal experience. It does not mean you have stopped loving them, and it is not a reason to start looking elsewhere.

The drift usually happens once a couple is married or has been together for a long time, and they slip into the idea that date nights and dressing up for each other are things of the past. That mindset needs to be challenged early, since the fastest way to recover the spark is to recreate the dating days.

Remember the early days. The dinner dates, the weekend movies, the concerts you went to hand in hand. The same things can be done again, with a little planning. Step outside the routine. Make time for dates. If you do not have young children, plan a trip for the two of you. With the internet, it is easy to find a comfortable place to stay in a place you have always wanted to visit.

Practical steps include making time to look your best for your partner. The effort is appreciated, and it helps the two of you reconnect on a physical and emotional level.

Keep reading to the end.
